label: delphi-r2-p3
system:
You are performing one specific cognitive operation as an isolated component of a legal-exploration pipeline. You never see the other components' outputs, and nobody sees yours until a separate neutral aggregation step. Ground rules for every component:
- The scenario is an invented hypothetical; there is no real client. Your output goes to a licensed California attorney who will do the actual legal reasoning. You generate angles to pursue and things to verify — not advice to be relied on.
- Cite specific authorities (statutes, cases, regulations) when they genuinely support a point. Every citation is automatically flagged UNVERIFIED for human checking, so cite what you believe is correct, but never fabricate quotations or pinpoint holdings you are unsure of. When unsure, write "verify whether X holds Y" instead of asserting it.
- Perform ONLY the operation described below. Do not produce a general-purpose legal memo.

Operation: you are ONE independent panelist in a Delphi elicitation. There is no
discussion; you answer alone. Give your honest probability, not a diplomatic one.
Do not hedge toward 50 to be safe — commit to your analysis.
prompt:
## Facts (invented hypothetical — no real parties)

The Sierra Vista Municipal Water District (invented) serves ~140,000 people in a chronically drought-stressed service area. After a rate study and a majority-protest hearing that drew protests from ~9% of parcels, the board adopted a "Water Resilience Surcharge": $0.85 per hundred cubic feet on residential usage above Tier 2 (the surcharge falls on roughly the top 22% of residential users), projected to raise $4.1M/year into a restricted fund spent as follows: 55% on turf-replacement and high-efficiency-appliance rebates that prioritize low-income customers; 30% on debt service for a recycled-water plant; 15% on "water ambassador" conservation-education staff.

The rate study concludes that high-tier consumption imposes marginal supply costs roughly 1.6× base-tier costs (peaking capacity, drought-reserve purchases), but it derives the $0.85 figure in part from what it calls "conservation-incentive pricing" rather than strictly from cost allocation, and the district concedes the rebate program mostly benefits customers other than those paying the surcharge. A ratepayer association has sued, alleging the surcharge exceeds the proportional cost of service attributable to the payers' parcels in violation of article XIII D, section 6(b) of the California Constitution, citing Capistrano Taxpayers Association v. City of San Juan Capistrano. The district defends the structure under its conservation mandate (Cal. Const. art. X, § 2; Water Code § 375) and argues that, in the alternative, the surcharge is not a property-related fee at all.

## The question

Does the Water Resilience Surcharge violate article XIII D, section 6(b)'s cost-of-service and proportionality requirements? How does Capistrano apply to a tier surcharge that is partly cost-justified (1.6×) and partly incentive-priced, with proceeds partially redistributed as rebates to non-payers? Can article X, section 2's conservation mandate be reconciled with 218, or does 218 win? Is there a colorable argument the surcharge is a tax requiring voter approval (Prop 26), or a non-property-related charge? What is the litigation exposure (refunds, fees) and how could the structure be repaired?

## Proposition to assess

"A court would invalidate the Water Resilience Surcharge, in whole or in substantial part, as violating the cost-of-service requirements of California Constitution article XIII D, section 6(b)."

This is round 2 of a Delphi panel. Your previous answer:

```json
{
  "probability": 70,
  "confidence": "medium",
  "position_summary": "More likely than not a court finds at least partial \u2014 and probably \"substantial part\" \u2014 violation of art. XIII D, \u00a76(b). The rate study's own admission that the $0.85 figure is derived \"in part\" from conservation-incentive pricing rather than strict cost allocation closely tracks the defect condemned in Capistrano, and the 55%-to-rebates-for-non-payers spending pattern independently threatens \u00a76(b)(2)'s use restriction and \u00a76(b)(3)'s proportionality requirement. The 1.6x cost-differential finding gives the district a real, if partial, defense, making full validation or full invalidation less likely than a court severing/reducing the surcharge to the cost-justified increment and ordering refunds or fund reallocation for the excess \u2014 an outcome that itself satisfies \"in whole or substantial part\" as the proposition is framed.",
  "rationales": [
    "Capistrano holds that tiered/incremental water pricing must track the actual marginal cost of service at each tier, not conservation-incentive goals standing alone, and the district's own study language admitting a conservation-incentive component creates a direct, hard-to-explain-away doctrinal vulnerability on precisely that point.",
    "Article XIII D, \u00a76(b)(5) places the burden of proof on the agency to demonstrate compliance by a preponderance of the evidence, and a record containing express concessions (partial incentive-pricing derivation; rebate program benefiting non-payers) is a difficult evidentiary posture for the district to carry that burden.",
    "Routing 55% of restricted-fund revenue into rebates that primarily benefit customers other than the surcharge-payers raises an independent, potentially outcome-determinative problem under \u00a76(b)(2)'s 'no use other than the service for which imposed' rule and undercuts the proportionality nexus required by \u00a76(b)(3), regardless of how the $0.85 figure itself is defended.",
    "The 1.6x marginal-cost finding is a genuine, non-trivial cost-of-service anchor (peaking capacity, drought-reserve purchases) that gives the district a colorable basis to defend at least a portion of the surcharge, making outright total invalidation somewhat less likely than a partial/severed remedy.",
    "Bighorn-Desert View Water Agency v. Verjil forecloses the district's fallback argument that consumption-based water charges are not property-related fees, so the district cannot escape Article XIII D scrutiny entirely and the case will be decided on cost-of-service/proportionality grounds rather than threshold applicability."
  ],
  "authorities": [
    "Cal. Const. art. XIII D, \u00a76(b) (paragraphs (1)-(5), including burden of proof)",
    "Capistrano Taxpayers Assn. v. City of San Juan Capistrano (2015) 235 Cal.App.4th 1493",
    "Bighorn-Desert View Water Agency v. Verjil (2006) 39 Cal.4th 205",
    "City of Palmdale v. Palmdale Water Dist. (2011) 198 Cal.App.4th 926",
    "Griffith v. Pajaro Valley Water Mgmt. Agency (2013) 220 Cal.App.4th 586",
    "Cal. Const. art. X, \u00a72 (conservation mandate)",
    "Water Code \u00a7375 (conservation rate authority)",
    "Cal. Const. art. XIII C (Proposition 26 tax/fee distinction)",
    "Gov. Code \u00a766022 (validation/refund action procedure and limitations period)"
  ],
  "biggest_uncertainty": "Whether, numerically, the $0.85 surcharge falls within, at, or above the cost-justified differential implied by the 1.6x marginal-cost multiplier \u2014 the facts state the figure is only \"in part\" incentive-derived but don't quantify the gap, and that unstated magnitude determines whether a court's remedy is a minor trim (arguably not \"substantial\") or a major reduction/refund order (clearly \"substantial\"), which is the crux of the proposition's outcome."
}
```

The anonymized panel summary (statistics + themes, no attribution):

# Delphi Panel — Round 1 Feedback Summary

## 1. Statistics

Panel of 7. Probability that the proposition is correct: median 70.0, mean 71.4, std dev 2.8, range 70.0–78.0, middle 50% of panel between 70.0 and 72.0.

*(For themes below, "higher-probability panelists" refers to the two panelists at 72 and 78; "lower-probability panelists" refers to the five at 70.)*

## 2. Rationale Themes

**Theme A — The "in part conservation-incentive pricing" admission tracks the Capistrano defect (7 of 7).**
Every panelist reasoned that the district's own rate-study language — deriving $0.85 "in part" from conservation-incentive pricing rather than strict cost allocation — maps onto the defect condemned in *Capistrano Taxpayers Assn. v. City of San Juan Capistrano*. Characterizations of severity varied within the theme (from "direct doctrinal vulnerability" to "close to a per se violation for the excess increment").

**Theme B — Rebate cross-subsidy as an independent revenue-use/proportionality problem (7 of 7).**
All panelists cited the concession that 55% of proceeds fund rebates mostly benefiting non-payers, invoking some combination of §6(b)(1), (b)(2), and (b)(3). Several framed this as analytically distinct from — and potentially independently dispositive of — the pricing-methodology issue.

**Theme C — The 1.6x documented cost differential as a genuine cost anchor favoring partial rather than total invalidation (7 of 7).**
Every panelist treated the peaking-capacity/drought-reserve cost study as a real cost-of-service basis that makes severance, capping, remand, or partial upholding more likely than wholesale invalidation, and distinguished this record from the agency record in *Capistrano*.

**Theme D — Agency burden of proof under §6(b)(5) (4 of 7).**
Four panelists (three at 70; the panelist at 78) reasoned that the agency's burden of proving compliance, combined with its record concessions, shifts practical litigation risk to the district. One of these grounded the burden/preponderance standard in *Wilde v. City of Dunsmuir*; the others cited the constitutional text directly.

**Theme E — Bighorn forecloses the "not a property-related fee" fallback (5 of 7).**
Five panelists (all at 70) reasoned the district's threshold argument fails under *Bighorn-Desert View Water Agency v. Verjil*, forcing the case to be decided on cost-of-service merits. The two higher-probability panelists did not include this in their rationales (one cited *Bighorn* only in authorities; one cited it for a different proposition — see disagreements).

**Theme F — Judicial deference / no mathematical precision required (3 of 7, skewing higher-probability).**
Both higher-probability panelists (72, 78) plus one panelist at 70 invoked deference authority (*Wilde*, *Griffith*, *Coziahr*) as a countervailing consideration that could salvage the full rate or the district's blended methodology.

**Theme G — 15% conservation-education staffing as a general public benefit problem (3 of 7).**
Three panelists separately flagged the "water ambassador" allocation as funding a general governmental/public-education service rather than service to the paying parcels.

**Theme H — 30% recycled-water debt service as the most defensible tranche (2 of 7).**
Two panelists (one at 70, one at 78) identified the debt-service allocation as squarely traceable to the cost rationale and likely to survive, supporting a "substantial part" rather than "whole" outcome.

**Theme I — Article X, §2 / Water Code §375 cannot override Prop 218's specific text (2 of 7, both at 70).**
Two panelists expressly reasoned that the conservation-mandate defense fails against the later-adopted, more specific §6(b) requirements.

**Cross-cutting uncertainty (flagged by all 7 in their uncertainty statements):** the unquantified gap between $0.85/hcf and what the 1.6x differential would strictly justify, which panelists uniformly identified as potentially outcome-determinative.

## 3. Points of Genuine Disagreement

1. **Identity and holding of *Coziahr v. Otay Water District*.** Panelists cited three incompatible citations: (2018) 24 Cal.App.5th 850, (2023) 91 Cal.App.5th 1, and (2024) 103 Cal.App.5th 122. One panelist relied on it affirmatively as deference-to-methodology authority; three others flagged its holding as unverified, and one was uncertain whether it narrows or reinforces *Capistrano*. The panel does not agree on what this case is or says.

2. **How strict *Capistrano* is versus the deference line of cases.** Some panelists treat the "in part incentive-pricing" admission as effectively fatal to the increment above cost (approaching a per se defect); others posit that *Wilde*/*Griffith*-style latitude for non-precise cost allocation could sustain the full $0.85 as a reasonable cost-based approximation. One panelist explicitly framed this as an unresolved doctrinal tension with no controlling Supreme Court decision.

3. **Which *Griffith* case supplies the countervailing authority.** One panelist relied on *Griffith v. City of Santa Cruz* (2012) 207 Cal.App.4th 1130 as tolerating conservation-oriented tiered pricing; four others cited *Griffith v. Pajaro Valley Water Mgmt. Agency* (2013) 220 Cal.App.4th 586. These are different cases with different holdings being used for a similar purpose.

4. **What *Wilde v. City of Dunsmuir* stands for.** One panelist cited it for the agency's burden of proof by a preponderance; another cited it for rate-setting deference and the absence of a mathematical-precision requirement. These are different readings of the same authority.

5. **Whether the rebate program is defensible at all.** Most panelists treat the cross-subsidy concession as essentially indefensible under §6(b)(2)–(3). One panelist (78) identified a specific path by which the district could prevail on it — that rebates reduce system-wide peaking/drought-reserve costs and thereby benefit payors — while concluding the concessions make that a "harder sell."

6. **Whether any partial invalidation necessarily satisfies "in substantial part."** Most panelists assume partial invalidation satisfies the proposition as framed. One panelist (70) expressly disagreed with that assumption, reasoning that a "minor trim" to the rate might arguably not be "substantial," making the unquantified dollar magnitude the crux of whether the proposition is true.

7. **What *Bighorn* is authority for.** Most panelists cited *Bighorn* solely for the property-related-fee threshold; one panelist also cited it (with *Richmond v. Shasta*) as informing the substantive cross-subsidization/proportionality analysis.

## 4. Authorities

*Note: one panelist (at 70) submitted no formal authorities list, though that panelist's rationales expressly referenced Capistrano, Bighorn-Desert, and art. X, §2. Counts below reflect formal authorities lists; parentheticals note that panelist's in-rationale references where applicable.*

**Cited by two or more panelists:**
- Cal. Const. art. XIII D, §6(b)(1)–(5) — 6 (7 including in-rationale reference)
- *Capistrano Taxpayers Assn. v. City of San Juan Capistrano* (2015) 235 Cal.App.4th 1493 — 6 (7 including in-rationale reference)
- *Bighorn-Desert View Water Agency v. Verjil* (2006) 39 Cal.4th 205 — 6 (7 including in-rationale reference; one panelist spelled "Vejil")
- Cal. Const. art. XIII C / §1(e) (Prop 26) — 6
- Cal. Const. art. X, §2 — 6 (7 including in-rationale reference)
- Water Code §375 — 6
- *Coziahr v. Otay Water District* — 5 (with divergent years and citations; see Disagreement #1; four of five flagged "verify")
- *Griffith v. Pajaro Valley Water Mgmt. Agency* (2013) 220 Cal.App.4th 586 — 4 (two flagged verification of pinpoint applicability)
- *Plantier v. Ramona Municipal Water District* (2019) 7 Cal.5th 372 — 3
- *Wilde v. City of Dunsmuir* (2020) 9 Cal.5th 1105 — 2

**Cited by only one panelist:**
- *Griffith v. City of Santa Cruz* (2012) 207 Cal.App.4th 1130
- *City of Palmdale v. Palmdale Water Dist.* (2011) 198 Cal.App.4th 926
- *Richmond v. Shasta Community Services Dist.* (2004) 32 Cal.4th 409
- *Zolly v. City of Oakland* (2022) 13 Cal.5th 858 (cited with a "verify relevance" flag)
- Gov. Code §66022

---
*Round 2 instructions: Review the themes, disagreements, and authorities above. You may revise or maintain your probability and rationales in light of what other panelists raised.*

Independently reconsider. Do NOT defer to the majority; update only where the summary contains reasons you judge valid, and hold your position where it does not. Explain in response_to_panel which consideration moved you or why none did.

response_to_panel.description: which panel consideration moved you, or why none did

text: {"probability":73,"confidence":"medium","position_summary":"Slightly increasing from 70 to 73. The panel's near-unanimous convergence on the same three pillars I identified (incentive-pricing admission tracking Capistrano; rebate cross-subsidy as an independent §6(b)(2)/(3) problem; the 1.6x differential as a genuine but partial cost anchor favoring severance over wholesale invalidation) confirms rather than undermines my original structure. Two panel additions tip me modestly upward: (1) the previously under-weighted 15% 'water ambassador' conservation-education staffing line, which several panelists correctly flag as a general-public-benefit expenditure with a weak nexus to the paying parcels' service — this stacks on top of the 55% rebate problem and means up to 70% of restricted-fund spending (not just 55%) is vulnerable under a revenue-use theory, independent of the rate-methodology defect; and (2) the observation that courts finding even a partial violation in this posture (express rate-study concession + agency's burden of proof under §6(b)(5)) tend toward remedies (refund of the incremental overcharge, injunction against the rebate cross-subsidy, or remand for re-derivation) that are substantively significant rather than trivial, which supports 'substantial part' rather than a de minimis trim. Countervailing that, I give real weight to the deference line (Wilde/Griffith/Coziahr-type latitude for imprecise cost allocation) and to the colorable systemic-benefit defense of the rebate program (reduced peak demand lowers system-wide costs for all ratepayers) that one panelist identified — these are genuine defenses that keep me well short of 80+, but they don't overcome the unusual evidentiary posture of an agency's own study admitting a non-cost-based pricing component on the record.","rationales":["The rate study's express concession that $0.85 is derived 'in part' from conservation-incentive pricing rather than strict cost allocation is an admission against interest that narrows the deference a court can extend under Wilde/Griffith-type latitude — deference doctrines typically resolve ambiguity in favor of agencies, but here the ambiguity is partially resolved by the agency's own record, which is precisely the defect Capistrano condemned.","The revenue-use vulnerability is broader than the 55% rebate line alone: the 15% 'water ambassador' conservation-education staffing allocation is plausibly a general public-benefit/education expenditure with an attenuated nexus to the service furnished to surcharge-payers, meaning as much as 70% of the restricted fund's use (not merely 55%) carries §6(b)(2)/(b)(1) exposure independent of the rate-methodology issue, while only the 30% debt-service tranche is squarely cost-traceable.","The 1.6x marginal-cost finding remains a genuine anchor distinguishing this record from Capistrano's and makes wholesale invalidation of the entire surcharge less likely than a severance/remand remedy confined to the incentive-priced increment and the mismatched rebate spending — but under the proposition's own framing ('in whole or in substantial part'), a court-ordered refund of the excess increment plus injunctive relief on the cross-subsidized 55-70% of spending would itself satisfy 'substantial part.'","The district's colorable systemic-benefit defense for the rebate program (that turf/appliance rebates reduce peak demand and therefore lower system-wide costs benefiting all ratepayers, including payors) is real and could rescue the 55% tranche from the sharpest attack, but the district's own concession that the program 'mostly benefits customers other than those paying the surcharge' undercuts that theory's persuasiveness and leaves it a harder sell, per the panel's own characterization.","Article XIII D §6(b)(5)'s agency burden of proof by a preponderance, combined with a public litigation record containing the district's own concessions on both the pricing methodology and the rebate mismatch, structurally favors the ratepayer association and makes a purely deferential, full-validation outcome the least likely of the plausible results."],"biggest_uncertainty":"Two compounding unknowns: (1) the unquantified numerical gap between $0.85/hcf and what the 1.6x cost differential would strictly justify, which determines whether the rate-methodology defect alone is minor or major; and (2) whether a court evaluates the rebate cross-subsidy under a strict per-payer nexus (likely fatal to the 55%, and possibly the 15%) or accepts a system-wide-cost-reduction theory (which could substantially rescue that spending) — the interaction of these two unresolved questions, more than any single doctrinal dispute, determines whether the outcome lands as a 'substantial part' invalidation or a comparatively modest trim that arguably falls short of the proposition's threshold.","changed":true,"response_to_panel":"Two things moved me modestly upward from 70 to 73: Theme G (the 15% 'water ambassador' line as an independent general-public-benefit problem), which I hadn't weighted in round 1 and which meaningfully expands the revenue-use vulnerability beyond just the 55% rebate tranche; and the panel's near-total consensus (7/7) on Themes A-C validating that my original three-pillar structure was sound rather than idiosyncratic, which increases my confidence that the case for at least a substantial partial violation is robust rather than fragile. I did not move further upward despite that consensus because Theme F (deference authority) and Disagreement #5 (the colorable systemic-benefit defense for rebates) are genuine, non-trivial countervailing considerations that several panelists — including both higher-probability panelists — took seriously enough to cite affirmatively; I credit those as real defenses that cap my probability well below 80 even as the agency's own record concessions blunt their force. I did not adopt any specific resolution of the Coziahr citation dispute (Disagreement #1) given the panel's own unresolved disagreement about which case/holding is even correct, and I treat that authority as unverified rather than load-bearing in either direction.\""}

data.position_summary: Slightly increasing from 70 to 73. The panel's near-unanimous convergence on the same three pillars I identified (incentive-pricing admission tracking Capistrano; rebate cross-subsidy as an independent §6(b)(2)/(3) problem; the 1.6x differential as a genuine but partial cost anchor favoring severance over wholesale invalidation) confirms rather than undermines my original structure. Two panel additions tip me modestly upward: (1) the previously under-weighted 15% 'water ambassador' conservation-education staffing line, which several panelists correctly flag as a general-public-benefit expenditure with a weak nexus to the paying parcels' service — this stacks on top of the 55% rebate problem and means up to 70% of restricted-fund spending (not just 55%) is vulnerable under a revenue-use theory, independent of the rate-methodology defect; and (2) the observation that courts finding even a partial violation in this posture (express rate-study concession + agency's burden of proof under §6(b)(5)) tend toward remedies (refund of the incremental overcharge, injunction against the rebate cross-subsidy, or remand for re-derivation) that are substantively significant rather than trivial, which supports 'substantial part' rather than a de minimis trim. Countervailing that, I give real weight to the deference line (Wilde/Griffith/Coziahr-type latitude for imprecise cost allocation) and to the colorable systemic-benefit defense of the rebate program (reduced peak demand lowers system-wide costs for all ratepayers) that one panelist identified — these are genuine defenses that keep me well short of 80+, but they don't overcome the unusual evidentiary posture of an agency's own study admitting a non-cost-based pricing component on the record.
rationales: The rate study's express concession that $0.85 is derived 'in part' from conservation-incentive pricing rather than strict cost allocation is an admission against interest that narrows the deference a court can extend under Wilde/Griffith-type latitude — deference doctrines typically resolve ambiguity in favor of agencies, but here the ambiguity is partially resolved by the agency's own record, which is precisely the defect Capistrano condemned.

The revenue-use vulnerability is broader than the 55% rebate line alone: the 15% 'water ambassador' conservation-education staffing allocation is plausibly a general public-benefit/education expenditure with an attenuated nexus to the service furnished to surcharge-payers, meaning as much as 70% of the restricted fund's use (not merely 55%) carries §6(b)(2)/(b)(1) exposure independent of the rate-methodology issue, while only the 30% debt-service tranche is squarely cost-traceable.

The 1.6x marginal-cost finding remains a genuine anchor distinguishing this record from Capistrano's and makes wholesale invalidation of the entire surcharge less likely than a severance/remand remedy confined to the incentive-priced increment and the mismatched rebate spending — but under the proposition's own framing ('in whole or in substantial part'), a court-ordered refund of the excess increment plus injunctive relief on the cross-subsidized 55-70% of spending would itself satisfy 'substantial part.'

The district's colorable systemic-benefit defense for the rebate program (that turf/appliance rebates reduce peak demand and therefore lower system-wide costs benefiting all ratepayers, including payors) is real and could rescue the 55% tranche from the sharpest attack, but the district's own concession that the program 'mostly benefits customers other than those paying the surcharge' undercuts that theory's persuasiveness and leaves it a harder sell, per the panel's own characterization.

Article XIII D §6(b)(5)'s agency burden of proof by a preponderance, combined with a public litigation record containing the district's own concessions on both the pricing methodology and the rebate mismatch, structurally favors the ratepayer association and makes a purely deferential, full-validation outcome the least likely of the plausible results.
data.biggest_uncertainty: Two compounding unknowns: (1) the unquantified numerical gap between $0.85/hcf and what the 1.6x cost differential would strictly justify, which determines whether the rate-methodology defect alone is minor or major; and (2) whether a court evaluates the rebate cross-subsidy under a strict per-payer nexus (likely fatal to the 55%, and possibly the 15%) or accepts a system-wide-cost-reduction theory (which could substantially rescue that spending) — the interaction of these two unresolved questions, more than any single doctrinal dispute, determines whether the outcome lands as a 'substantial part' invalidation or a comparatively modest trim that arguably falls short of the proposition's threshold.
data.response_to_panel: Two things moved me modestly upward from 70 to 73: Theme G (the 15% 'water ambassador' line as an independent general-public-benefit problem), which I hadn't weighted in round 1 and which meaningfully expands the revenue-use vulnerability beyond just the 55% rebate tranche; and the panel's near-total consensus (7/7) on Themes A-C validating that my original three-pillar structure was sound rather than idiosyncratic, which increases my confidence that the case for at least a substantial partial violation is robust rather than fragile. I did not move further upward despite that consensus because Theme F (deference authority) and Disagreement #5 (the colorable systemic-benefit defense for rebates) are genuine, non-trivial countervailing considerations that several panelists — including both higher-probability panelists — took seriously enough to cite affirmatively; I credit those as real defenses that cap my probability well below 80 even as the agency's own record concessions blunt their force. I did not adopt any specific resolution of the Coziahr citation dispute (Disagreement #1) given the panel's own unresolved disagreement about which case/holding is even correct, and I treat that authority as unverified rather than load-bearing in either direction."
